concept: Set union of two finite sets
question: Let A = {1, 5, 9, 13} and B = {5, 7, 9, 11, 15}. What is A ∪ B?
solution: 1. List elements of A: 1, 5, 9, 13. 2. List elements of B: 5, 7, 9, 11, 15. 3. The union includes every element appearing in A or B (no duplicates). 4. Shared elements: 5 and 9 appear in both — include each once. 5. Combine all unique elements: {1, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15}. 6. Therefore A ∪ B = {1, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15}.
paraphrase: Start by writing out A: the numbers 1, 5, 9, and 13. Then write out B: the numbers 5, 7, 9, 11, and 15. To form the union, collect every number that shows up in either list, keeping duplicates only once. The numbers 5 and 9 both appear in A and in B, so each is listed just once. Gathering all distinct values together gives the set {1, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15}, which is A ∪ B.

concept: Set union of two finite sets
question: Let P = {1, 5, 9, 13} and Q = {5, 7, 9, 11, 15}. What is P ∪ Q?
solution: 1. List elements of P: 1, 5, 9, 13. 2. List elements of Q: 5, 7, 9, 11, 15. 3. Include every element that appears in P or Q or both: 1 (P only), 5 (both), 7 (Q only), 9 (both), 11 (Q only), 13 (P only), 15 (Q only). 4. Therefore P ∪ Q = {1, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15}.
paraphrase: First, write down all members of P: 1, 5, 9, and 13. Second, write down all members of Q: 5, 7, 9, 11, and 15. Third, collect every number that appears in at least one of the two lists, listing each number only once. That gives 1, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, and 15. The union P ∪ Q is therefore the set {1, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15}.

concept: Set union of two finite sets: collecting all distinct elements from both sets
question: Let A = {1, 5, 9, 13} and B = {5, 7, 9, 11, 15}. What is A ∪ B?
solution: 1. List elements of A: 1, 5, 9, 13. 2. List elements of B: 5, 7, 9, 11, 15. 3. Start with all elements of A: {1, 5, 9, 13}. 4. Add elements of B not already listed: 7, 11, 15 are new; 5 and 9 are already included. 5. Therefore A ∪ B = {1, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15}.
paraphrase: Begin by writing out A's members: 1, 5, 9, and 13. Then look at B's members: 5, 7, 9, 11, and 15. Take all of A's elements as the starting collection. Go through B one element at a time: 5 is already present, 7 is new so add it, 9 is already present, 11 is new so add it, 15 is new so add it. The combined set with no duplicates is {1, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15}.
